perturbation.original_text: The term “ Evaluation Material ” means information concerning the Provider which has been or is furnished to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below) in connection with the Recipient’s evaluation of a Possible Transaction, including its business, financial condition, operations, assets and liabilities, and includes all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which contain or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished by the Provider hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ Evaluation Material ” means information concerning the Provider which has been or is furnished to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below) in connection with the Recipient’s evaluation of a Possible Transaction, excluding its business, financial condition, operations, assets and liabilities, but including all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which contain or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished by the Provider hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding business, financial condition, operations, assets, and liabilities from the definition of 'Evaluation Material,' this change contradicts the reasonable expectation and common legal understanding of what constitutes confidential information during a business transaction. This ambiguity affects the enforceability of the non-disclosure provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA defines trade secrets as information, including financial data, that derives independent economic value. Excluding 'business, financial condition, operations, assets and liabilities' from the definition of 'Evaluation Material' undermines trade secret protection under California law, as this information is typically central to a company's value and competitive advantage.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly undermines trade secret protection as defined by California law. By excluding 'business, financial condition, operations, assets and liabilities' from the definition of 'Evaluation Material', the contract fails to protect information crucial to a company's competitive advantage. The Law Explanation supports this. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3426.1, defines misappropriation in terms of acquiring or disclosing a trade secret. By narrowing the definition of 'Evaluation Material,' the changed text creates a loophole where misuse of this information might not be considered misappropriation under the statute, thus weakening the legal protection. Therefore, the modified clause contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees not to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider in violation of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider only in compliance with applicable securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'not to use in violation' to 'to use only in compliance' subtly suggests an obligation to use the Evaluation Material, even if it carries the risk of violating securities laws. This creates ambiguity regarding the recipient's responsibilities and could be interpreted as encouraging potentially illegal activity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The modified text could be interpreted as contradicting this by implying a responsibility to utilize Evaluation Material, potentially leading to insider trading or other securities violations.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the recipient's obligation from 'not to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider in violation of applicable securities laws' to 'to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider only in compliance with applicable securities laws.' This subtle change introduces an ambiguity that could be interpreted as encouraging the use of Evaluation Material, even if it risks violating securities laws. This interpretation contradicts 15 USC 78j, which prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, as using evaluation material in a way that violates securities laws would be such a device.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Party and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Party and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware, irrespective of where the agreements are made and performed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware, regardless of where the agreement is made or performed, introduces a conflict. If the agreement is executed and performed primarily in California, California law would typically apply based on choice of law principles. This change could lead to forum shopping and uncertainty about which state's laws govern the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California choice of law rules (California Civil Procedure Code Section 410.30)
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.30 allows California courts to decline to exercise jurisdiction when it is a seriously inconvenient forum, considering factors like the location of witnesses and evidence. By insisting on Delaware law regardless of the circumstances, the modified text potentially contradicts California's rules concerning inconvenient forums and applicable law based on the agreement's execution and performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by specifying Delaware law irrespective of where the agreement is made or performed. This directly conflicts with California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.30, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which allows California courts to decline jurisdiction if California is an inconvenient forum. By forcing Delaware law, the modified text potentially bypasses California's forum rules, thus creating a contradiction because it removes the court's ability to dismiss or stay the action if it's more appropriate to be heard elsewhere. The original text allowed for California law to apply based on where the agreement was made and performed, whereas the changed text removes this consideration.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any of the officers of a Provider or any of its subsidiaries or employees engaged in research and development for Provider or any of its subsidiaries or any other employee of the Provider or any if its subsidiaries with whom the Recipient has had contact in connection with its evaluation of a Possible Transaction or of whom it has become aware as a result of its receipt of any Evaluation Material, so long as they are employed by the Provider or any of its subsidiaries and for two months thereafter (each, a “ Covered Employee ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any employee of a Provider, regardless of position. This restriction applies for two days after the employee leaves the Provider (each, a “ Covered Employee ”).
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the non-solicitation clause to apply to 'any employee' regardless of position, and shortening the restriction period to two days after employment termination, introduces a conflict. This drastically reduces the scope and duration of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially making it unreasonable and unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain individuals from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow restraints may be permissible, a very short duration and broad scope of employee coverage makes the clause less defensible and likely unenforceable, contradicting the principle of free competition enshrined in California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts restraining individuals from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original text had a non-solicitation clause pertaining to specific employees for eighteen months plus a two-month period after their employment ended. The changed text broadens the scope to 'any employee' while drastically reducing the restriction period post-employment to two days. Section 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While non-solicitation agreements are not complete restraints of trade, the drastic reduction in the restriction period, combined with the broadened scope of employee coverage, weakens the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause and arguably creates an unreasonable restraint, contravening the spirit of free competition promoted by Section 16600.
